examples.title: Linear Search - O(n) Time Complexity
examples.description: Demonstrates linear time complexity where execution time grows linearly with input size. The algorithm checks each element sequentially until the target is found.
examples.code:
def linear_search(arr, target):
    """
    Linear Search Algorithm
    Best Case: O(1) - when target is at first position
    Worst Case: O(n) - when target is at last position or not found
    Average Case: O(n)
    """
    for i in range(len(arr)):
        if arr[i] == target:
            return i  # Target found at index i
    return -1  # Target not found

# Test the function
data = [10, 25, 33, 47, 58, 62, 79, 85, 91, 100]
target = 62
result = linear_search(data, target)
print(f"Element {target} found at index: {result}")

# Test with non-existent element
target = 50
result = linear_search(data, target)
print(f"Element {target} found at index: {result}")
examples.explanation: The function iterates through the array exactly n times in the worst case. Each iteration performs constant-time operations (comparison). Therefore, time complexity is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) as only a few variables are used regardless of input size.

examples.title: Bubble Sort - O(n²) Time Complexity
examples.description: Demonstrates quadratic time complexity using nested loops. Bubble sort repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if they are in wrong order, causing the runtime to grow quadratically with input size.
examples.code:
def bubble_sort(arr):
    """
    Bubble Sort Algorithm
    Best Case: O(n) - when array is already sorted (with optimization)
    Worst Case: O(n²) - when array is reverse sorted
    Average Case: O(n²)
    """
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(n):
        # Optimization: track if any swap occurred
        swapped = False
        for j in range(0, n - i - 1):
            if arr[j] > arr[j + 1]:
                # Swap adjacent elements
                arr[j], arr[j + 1] = arr[j + 1], arr[j]
                swapped = True
        # If no swapping occurred, array is already sorted
        if not swapped:
            break
    return arr

# Test the function
data = [64, 34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 90]
print(f"Original array: {data}")
sorted_data = bubble_sort(data.copy())
print(f"Sorted array: {sorted_data}")

# Trace through first pass
data_trace = [5, 1, 4, 2, 8]
print(f"\nTracing first pass on {data_trace}:")
for j in range(len(data_trace) - 1):
    if data_trace[j] > data_trace[j + 1]:
        data_trace[j], data_trace[j + 1] = data_trace[j + 1], data_trace[j]
        print(f"  After swap: {data_trace}")
examples.explanation: Bubble sort uses two nested loops. The outer loop runs n times, and the inner loop runs n-i-1 times for each iteration i. This results in n*(n-1)/2 comparisons, which simplifies to O(n²). With the optimization (early exit), best case becomes O(n) when array is already sorted. Space complexity is O(1) as sorting is done in-place.

examples.title: Binary Search - O(log n) Time Complexity
examples.description: Demonstrates logarithmic time complexity, one of the most efficient algorithms. Binary search divides the search space in half with each iteration, making it ideal for sorted arrays.
examples.code:
def binary_search(arr, target):
    """
    Binary Search Algorithm (Iterative)
    Best Case: O(1) - when target is at middle position
    Worst Case: O(log n) - when target is not found
    Average Case: O(log n)
    
    Prerequisite: Array must be sorted
    """
    left, right = 0, len(arr) - 1
    
    while left <= right:
        # Calculate middle index (avoids overflow in other languages)
        mid = left + (right - left) // 2
        
        # Check if target is at mid
        if arr[mid] == target:
            return mid
        # If target is greater, ignore left half
        elif arr[mid] < target:
            left = mid + 1
        # If target is smaller, ignore right half
        else:
            right = mid - 1
    
    return -1  # Target not found

def binary_search_recursive(arr, target, left=None, right=None):
    """Recursive version of Binary Search"""
    if left is None:
        left = 0
    if right is None:
        right = len(arr) - 1
    
    if left > right:
        return -1
    
    mid = left + (right - left) // 2
    
    if arr[mid] == target:
        return mid
    elif arr[mid] < target:
        return binary_search_recursive(arr, target, mid + 1, right)
    else:
        return binary_search_recursive(arr, target, left, mid - 1)

# Test the function with sorted data
sorted_data = [2, 3, 4, 10, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90]
target = 40

result = binary_search(sorted_data, target)
print(f"Iterative - Element {target} found at index: {result}")

result_recursive = binary_search_recursive(sorted_data, target)
print(f"Recursive - Element {target} found at index: {result_recursive}")

# Demonstrate logarithmic reduction
print("\nDemonstrating log n reduction:")
n = 16
iterations = 0
while n >= 1:
    iterations += 1
    print(f"  Iteration {iterations}: Search space size = {n}")
    n = n // 2
print(f"Total iterations for n=16: {iterations} (log₂16 = 4)")
examples.explanation: Binary search works on the divide-and-conquer principle. With each iteration, the search space is reduced by half. If we start with n elements, after k iterations we have n/2^k elements. We stop when n/2^k = 1, giving us k = log₂n. This makes time complexity O(log n). For n=16, only 4 iterations are needed (log₂16 = 4). Space complexity is O(1) for iterative version and O(log n) for recursive version due to call stack.
